title: Build a Business with AI: From Idea to Launch
description: Learn to build a real business using AI as your co-founder. Not a toy project — a business that serves real people, solves real problems, and generates real revenue. Every lesson produces a deliverable you can ship. Because the cost of starting has dropped 99.7%, and the only thing between you and your first customer is the decision to start.

lessons.title: Find a Problem Worth Solving
objectives: Use AI to brainstorm problems in your daily life, community, and industry
Evaluate problems by urgency, frequency, and willingness-to-pay
Write a one-sentence problem statement that passes the 'mom test'
Identify 3 people who have this problem and would talk to you about it
hints: The best businesses solve problems the founder personally experiences
Ask AI: 'What are 20 problems faced by [your profession/community] that nobody is solving well?'
A good problem statement: '[Who] struggles with [what] because [why], and it costs them [how much]'
assessmentCriteria: Problem statement is specific (not vague like 'help people')
Target customer is clearly identified
Problem frequency is daily or weekly (not once a year)
Student can articulate why existing solutions fail

lessons.title: Talk to Real Humans (The Mom Test)
objectives: Learn why asking 'would you use this?' gives you wrong answers
Write interview questions that reveal real behavior, not opinions
Use AI to analyze interview notes and extract patterns
Build a simple customer insight document from 3 conversations
hints: Never ask 'would you buy this?' — instead ask 'how do you solve this problem today?'
The Mom Test: talk about their life, not your idea. Your mom will lie to you to be nice.
After each conversation, ask AI to extract: pain level (1-10), current solution, money spent
assessmentCriteria: Interview script avoids leading questions
At least 3 insights come from real conversations, not assumptions
Customer insight document separates facts from opinions
Student identifies a willingness-to-pay signal

modules.title: Build Your MVP in a Weekend

lessons.title: Design the Smallest Thing That Proves Your Idea
objectives: Distinguish between an MVP and a prototype (MVP generates revenue or validated learning)
Use AI to generate a feature list, then cut it to the 3 features that matter
Write user stories for your MVP: 'As a [who], I want [what], so that [why]'
Choose your stack: static site, React app, or API-first
hints: An MVP is not a crappy version of your product. It's the smallest experiment that tests your riskiest assumption.
Ask AI: 'I want to build [X]. What is the ONE feature that, if it doesn't work, means the whole idea fails?'
If you can solve the problem with a spreadsheet and a phone, do that first
assessmentCriteria: MVP scope is achievable in 1-2 days, not months
Core value proposition is testable with the MVP
User stories follow the correct format
Student can explain what they'll learn from launching the MVP

lessons.title: Build It: AI as Your Engineering Team
objectives: Use AI coding assistants to generate a working landing page with signup
Deploy your site to a free hosting platform (Vercel, Netlify, or GitHub Pages)
Add a payment link or waitlist form that captures real intent
Set up basic analytics to track who visits and what they click
hints: Tell the AI: 'Build me a landing page for [product] that explains [value prop] and has a [signup/buy] button'
You don't need to understand every line of code. You need to understand what it does and why.
Deploy early, deploy often. A live page with 10 visitors teaches more than a perfect page with zero.
assessmentCriteria: Landing page is deployed and accessible via a real URL
Page clearly communicates the problem and solution
There is a working call-to-action (signup, waitlist, or purchase)
Analytics are capturing at least page views

lessons.title: Get Your First Customer
objectives: Share your MVP with the people you interviewed in Lesson 2
Use AI to write outreach messages that don't sound like spam
Track responses and iterate based on feedback
Celebrate or pivot: did someone pay or sign up?
hints: Your first customer is probably someone you already know. Go back to your interview list.
Don't pitch. Share: 'Remember that problem we talked about? I built something. Want to try it?'
If nobody signs up, that's data. Ask why. The answer is your next iteration.
assessmentCriteria: MVP was shared with at least 5 real people
At least one person took a meaningful action (signup, payment, or detailed feedback)
Student documented what worked and what didn't
Student has a clear next step: double down or pivot

modules.title: Grow with AI as Your Team

lessons.title: Run Your Business with AI Agents
objectives: Set up AI for customer support (answering FAQs, routing issues)
Use AI to write marketing content that sounds like you, not a robot
Automate repetitive tasks: invoicing, scheduling, follow-ups
Build a simple dashboard to track your key metrics
hints: Start with the task you do most often that doesn't require judgment. Automate that first.
AI customer support should escalate to you for anything it's not 95% confident about
Your key metrics are: visitors, signups, active users, revenue. Track nothing else at first.
assessmentCriteria: At least one operational task is automated with AI
Marketing content passes the anti-AI-language test
Student can explain their unit economics (cost to serve one customer)
Dashboard shows real data, not demo data

lessons.title: Scale What Works, Kill What Doesn't
objectives: Use data to decide: scale, pivot, or stop
Write a one-page business plan using AI (not a 50-page deck nobody reads)
Identify your next 3 growth experiments
Plan your path: bootstrap, raise capital, or stay small by design
hints: If you have paying customers and positive unit economics, scale. If not, pivot or stop.
A one-page business plan: Problem, Solution, Customer, Revenue Model, Next 3 Moves.
Most successful businesses started as side projects. You don't have to quit your job.
assessmentCriteria: Decision to scale/pivot/stop is backed by data, not feelings
One-page business plan is complete and coherent
Growth experiments are specific and measurable
Student has a realistic 90-day action plan
